title: The Secret Ingredient They Don’t Want You to Know About

chunks.title: Introduction to EPG and its Origins
chunks.summary: The discussion begins with a new ingredient found in protein bars called EPG asterified propoxillated glycerol, which is likened to the old ingredient Ostra. The speaker describes the processing of canola oil to create an indigestible form of fat that mimics the texture of fat while causing gastrointestinal issues.
chunks.transcript: Have you heard about this this new ingredient that's in some of these protein bars? EPG asterified propoxillated glycerol. It's this it's interesting. Do you remember Ostra? >> Of course. Yeah. >> Okay. You remember anal leakage? >> Yeah. One of the side effects. >> Yes. So Ostro was sort of version 1.0 of this indigestible form of fat. And there's a company called Epig or EPA that in 2019 applied for and got granted this generally recognized as safe designation from the FDI for FDA for EPG asterified propoxilated glycerol. Basically, they take canola oil and they separate the fatty acid tails from the glycerol head with a propoxal molecule and it makes something that's been described by this company as the texture

chunks.title: Health Implications of EPG
chunks.summary: The speaker highlights the negative health implications of EPG, including gastrointestinal side effects and reduced absorption of fat-soluble vitamins such as beta carotene and vitamin K. Concerns are raised about the use of EPG in high-protein processed foods that are marketed to be healthy but may actually be detrimental.
chunks.transcript: feel of fat, but you can't digest it. That's the same thing that happened with Ostra. So when they've done studies with EPG, it causes all sorts of GI side effects like they in the papers it'll say anal spotting gas with discharge like akin to the alstra stuff. And over an 8week study it did appreciably decrease absorption of fats soluble vitamins like beta carotene and vitamin K1. They didn't test for vitamin K2 but surely it decreased vitamin K2 also. So, what's scary here is that I think this is the next incarnation of these processed foods. And again, it's all based on this calorie model, right? >> Calories in, calories out. They have this they're touting this bar as the highest calories per gram. chunks.title: Critique of High-Protein Processed Foods
chunks.summary: The video critiques products like the David Bar, which claims to provide high protein content with minimal calories using EPG. The speaker emphasizes the deceptive marketing tactics of such foods, which are ultraprocessed and lacking micronutrients despite appearing healthy.
chunks.transcript: are packaged like a wolf in sheep's clothing to be like, "Hey, that that the marketing on the David Bar literally says this is protein perfected." Like something isn't perfect, but the David Bar is. Like it's perfect protein. And again, they're appealing to this perspective, which is get more protein. Okay, we can talk about that, but at the lowest amount of calories possible. And of course, it's ultrarocessed. So, there's essentially no micronutrients. It's full of artificial flavors. It has sucralose, which I want to talk about also. So, it just worries me because this is the direction that convenience foods are going in. chunks.title: Future of EPG in Food Products
chunks.summary: Discussion shifts to the potential future prevalence of EPG in convenience foods following a lawsuit concerning monopolization in its supply. The speaker expresses concern for the broader implications of using EPG in everyday food products as consumer demand for convenience continues to rise.

I think we're going to see in the future once this lawsuit gets settled, a bunch of foods have this EPG in it. I want

chunks.title: The Dilemma of Convenience Foods
chunks.summary: The speaker reflects on the consumer need for convenience foods, particularly in settings like airports where healthy options are limited. There's a concern about how companies respond to this demand with lower quality food products that are misrepresented as healthy choices.
chunks.transcript: this is because it's basically like I said, it's depleting you of micronutrients and it's completely the wrong the wrong the wrong way to go with your your food. I mean, if you want high protein, low calories, eat a chicken breast, eat a piece of sirloin steak, right? Like that's so much better. But I get it. People want convenience. And so it just I think that there will always be this this niche for convenience foods and I think that people will always be reaching for and grabbing those convenience foods. It's all these convenience foods and I'm thinking like what is what what what are even the good options here? What is the best option in an airport? It's pretty tough.
